questions.q: By which constitutional amendment was the Anti-Defection Law introduced?
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law was introduced by the 52nd Amendment.

questions.q: In which year was the Anti-Defection Law introduced?
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law was introduced in 1985.

questions.q: The Anti-Defection Law was added to the Constitution as which Schedule?
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law was added as the Tenth Schedule to the Constitution.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the Anti-Defection Law?
options: To disqualify legislators who switch parties
questions.explanation: The law aims to disqualify legislators who defect from their party, curbing political defections.

questions.q: The Anti-Defection Law is commonly referred to by which name?
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law is contained in and commonly called the Tenth Schedule.

questions.q: What broad objective did the Anti-Defection Law seek to achieve for governments?
questions.explanation: The law aims to bring stability to governments by curbing defections.

questions.q: The Anti-Defection Law applies to members of which bodies?
questions.explanation: The Tenth Schedule applies to members of both Parliament and state legislatures.

questions.q: A legislator who voluntarily gives up membership of his or her party may face what consequence under the law?
questions.explanation: Voluntarily giving up party membership can lead to disqualification under the Tenth Schedule.

questions.q: In which decade was the Anti-Defection Law enacted?
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law was enacted in 1985, within the 1980s.

questions.q: The Anti-Defection Law addresses the practice of legislators doing what?
questions.explanation: The law targets the practice of legislators switching parties, known as defection.

questions.q: Under the Tenth Schedule, who decides questions of disqualification on the ground of defection in a House?
options: The presiding officer of the House
questions.explanation: The presiding officer (Speaker or Chairman) of the House decides questions of disqualification under the Tenth Schedule.

questions.q: The 52nd Amendment that introduced the Anti-Defection Law also amended provisions relating to which articles concerning disqualification?
questions.explanation: The 52nd Amendment amended Articles 101, 102, 190 and 191 relating to disqualification of legislators.

questions.q: Under which Prime Minister's government was the 52nd Amendment introducing the Anti-Defection Law enacted?
questions.explanation: The 52nd Amendment was enacted in 1985 during the government headed by Rajiv Gandhi.

questions.q: The original Tenth Schedule permitted a 'split' in a party if at least what fraction of members agreed?
questions.explanation: The original Tenth Schedule exempted a 'split' involving at least one-third of the legislature party's members.

questions.q: Which later constitutional amendment removed the 'split' exemption from the Anti-Defection Law?
questions.explanation: The 91st Amendment of 2003 removed the provision that protected a one-third 'split.'

questions.q: Under the law as amended, a 'merger' of a party is recognised if at least what fraction of members agree to it?
questions.explanation: A merger is exempted from disqualification if at least two-thirds of the members of the legislature party agree.

questions.q: Voting or abstaining contrary to a party direction (whip) can lead to disqualification unless what occurs?
options: The party condones it within the prescribed period
questions.explanation: Such voting attracts disqualification unless the party condones it within fifteen days under the Tenth Schedule.

questions.q: The Anti-Defection Law's provisions are designed to operate alongside which principle of the Council of Ministers?
questions.explanation: Party discipline under the Tenth Schedule supports the collective responsibility of the Council of Ministers.

questions.q: Does the Anti-Defection Law apply to a nominated member of a House?
options: No, nominated members are wholly exempt
Yes, with conditions on joining a party after a set period
questions.explanation: A nominated member may be disqualified if he or she joins a political party after six months from taking the seat.

questions.q: The Anti-Defection Law was a response to political instability that became prominent from which earlier period?
questions.explanation: Frequent defections from the late 1960s onward prompted the eventual enactment of the law in 1985.

questions.q: An independent member elected to a House faces disqualification under the Tenth Schedule if he or she does what?
options: Joins a political party after the election
questions.explanation: An independent member is disqualified if he or she joins any political party after being elected.

questions.q: The 91st Amendment of 2003, related to anti-defection, also placed a limit on the size of which body?
questions.explanation: The 91st Amendment capped the size of the Council of Ministers at fifteen percent of the House strength.

questions.q: Which 1992 Supreme Court case held that the presiding officer's decision under the Tenth Schedule is subject to judicial review?
questions.explanation: The Kihoto Hollohan case (1992) held that decisions under the Tenth Schedule are subject to judicial review.

questions.q: In deciding disqualification matters under the Tenth Schedule, the presiding officer is regarded as functioning in what capacity?
options: As a member of the executive
As an arbitrator chosen by parties
questions.explanation: The Kihoto Hollohan case held that the presiding officer acts as a tribunal when deciding such questions.

questions.q: The 91st Amendment provided that a member disqualified on the ground of defection is also barred from holding what until certain conditions are met?
options: A remunerative political post or office of profit
questions.explanation: The 91st Amendment barred a defector from holding a remunerative political post until re-elected or the term ends.

questions.q: The 52nd Amendment introducing the Anti-Defection Law came into force in which year?
questions.explanation: The 52nd Amendment was enacted and came into force in 1985.

questions.q: Under the Tenth Schedule, the conduct of the presiding officer when he or she himself ceases to belong to a party is governed by a special rule because the office requires what?
questions.explanation: The Tenth Schedule contains a special provision recognising the impartial nature of the presiding officer's role.

questions.q: The original protection for a 'split' that the 91st Amendment removed had been criticised because it could legitimise what?
options: Group defections by one-third of members
questions.explanation: The 'split' provision allowed group defections by one-third of members, which the 91st Amendment removed.

questions.q: Decisions of the presiding officer under the Tenth Schedule can ordinarily be challenged before which courts?
options: The High Courts and Supreme Court
questions.explanation: Following Kihoto Hollohan, such decisions are amenable to judicial review by the High Courts and the Supreme Court.

questions.q: The Anti-Defection Law balances party discipline against which competing consideration often debated in relation to it?
options: The fixing of the voting age
The freedom of speech and dissent of individual legislators
questions.explanation: Debates on the law concern the tension between party discipline and an individual legislator's freedom of dissent.
